perturbation.original_text: 4. The Receiving Party may use the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information for the sole purpose of evaluating, negotiating and, in its discr etion, entering into the proposed Transaction. A Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information pursuant to a request by an applicable regulatory authority, or if they are required by law or pursuant to an order of a court of competent jurisdiction to disclose Confidential Information, provided, however, in such event the Receiving Party shall (i) promptly notify the Disclosing Party; (ii) consult with the Disclosing Party as to the advisability of taking steps to resist or narrow such request; and ( iii) if so requested, cooperate with the Disclosing Party in seeking a protective or der or other appropriate remedy.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. The provisions of this Agreement shall remain in full force and effect beginning as of the Effective Date and shall continue with respect to a Receiving Party for as long as it retains the Confidential Information in its possession and for three (3) years thereafter. 4. The Receiving Party may use the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information for the sole purpose of evaluating, negotiating and, in its discr etion, entering into the proposed Transaction. A Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information pursuant to a request by an applicable regulatory authority, or if they are required by law or pursuant to an order of a court of competent jurisdiction to disclose Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the conditions of providing the Disclosing Party prompt notification and allowing them to seek a protective order when the Receiving Party is asked to disclose Confidential Information and placing it in the section about the term of the agreement reduces the transparency and enforceability of these obligations. The Disclosing party is less likely to learn of the disclosure.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Obligations of Confidentiality under Australian Law.
perturbation.law_explanation: By omitting the required steps of informing the Disclosing Party of disclosure requests and cooperating to seek remedies, this weakens the NDA's assurance of confidentiality. This contradicts principles of fair dealing and potentially misleads the Disclosing Party about the extent of protection afforded to their confidential information under Australian Consumer Law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Federal Register of Legislation Home Page Skip to main Page not found The page you requested could not be found. We've recently updated our website. perturbation.justification: The original contract requires the Receiving Party to promptly notify the Disclosing Party and cooperate in seeking a protective order if compelled to disclose confidential information. The changed text removes these obligations but places a section addressing the term of the agreement before the clause regarding the use of confidential information. The Perturbation Explanation notes this reduces the transparency and enforceability of these obligations, making it less likely for the Disclosing Party to learn of the disclosure. The Law Explanation suggests this weakens the NDA's assurance of confidentiality and potentially contradicts principles of fair dealing under Australian Consumer Law. This aligns with the 'Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction' definition, where a required legal disclosure is placed in an irrelevant section. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is a 'Page not found' error, the remaining information points to a contradiction because the modification reduces the protections afforded to the Disclosing Party, especially concerning their ability to protect their confidential information when disclosure is compelled by law or regulation.

perturbation.original_text: 3. The Receiving Party agrees (i) to adopt measures to protect the confidentiality, limited use and proprietary nature of the Confidential Information at least as restrictive as those it adopts for its own confidential information of similar importance and in any event, no less than reasonable; (ii) to disclose Confidential Information to only those of its directors, officers, agents, employees, corporate affiliates and third parties retained by the Receiving Party who have a need to know such information in order for the Receiving Party to accomplish the purposes set forth in Paragraph 4 hereof and who are made aware of the confidentiality provisions of this Agreement ( and in the case of a third party, who have signed a binding undertaking of confidentiality and non -use substantially equivalent to this Agreement ), and (iii) to not use Confidential Information for any purpose except as permitted by Paragraph 4. A Receiving Party shall be responsible to the Disclosing Party for any unauthorized u se or disclosure of Confidential Information by any party to whom Receiving Party has disclosed such information.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. This Agreement expresses the entire agreement between the Parties and supersedes all prior understandings and discussions between the Parties. 3. The Receiving Party agrees (i) to adopt measures to protect the confidentiality, limited use and proprietary nature of the Confidential Information at least as restrictive as those it adopts for its own confidential information of similar importance and in any event, no less than reasonable; (ii) to disclose Confidential Information to only those of its directors, officers, agents, employees, corporate affiliates and third parties retained by the Receiving Party who have a need to know such information in order for the Receiving Party to accomplish the purposes set forth in Paragraph 4 hereof and who are made aware of the confidentiality provisions of this Agreement ( and in the case of a third party, who have signed a binding undertaking of confidentiality and non -use substantially equivalent to this Agreement ), and (iii) to not use Confidential Information for any purpose except as permitted by Paragraph 4.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the sentence 'A Receiving Party shall be responsible to the Disclosing Party for any unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information by any party to whom Receiving Party has disclosed such information' from the paragraph that explicitly details the obligations for protecting confidential information and putting it in the 'entire agreement' section decreases its significance. Makes it harder to claim that the Receiving party is liable.
perturbation.law_explanation: The altered NDA diminishes the explicit assignment of responsibility to the Receiving Party for unauthorized disclosures, this could potentially undermine the principle of vicarious liability, where one party is held responsible for the actions of others under their control. This creates ambiguity in holding the Receiving Party accountable, thus contradicting common law precedents regarding duty of care and responsibility.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Hollingdale v North Coast Area Health Service (No.2) [2006] FMCA 585 - BarNet Jade
perturbation.justification: The change removes the explicit assignment of responsibility to the Receiving Party for unauthorized disclosures by parties to whom they disclosed confidential information. The Law Explanation suggests this undermines the principle of vicarious liability and contradicts common law precedents regarding duty of care and responsibility. The Perturbation Explanation argues that this weakens the Receiving Party's liability. While there is no specific scraped law snippet to directly support this, the removal of the sentence, as detailed in the Perturbation Explanation and analyzed in the Law Explanation, weakens the contractual obligation. Given this removal and the potential impact on liability, as well as the Structural Flaws - Legal Contradiction definition, the change establishes a notable legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 5. The Parties shall promptly advise each other in writing if they learn of any unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information by any of their directors, officers, employees, corporate affiliates, agents or by any third party.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. This Agreement may be executed in one or more counterparts, each of which shall be deemed an original, but all of which together constitute one and the same instrument. 5. The Parties shall promptly advise each other in writing if they learn of any unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information by any of their directors, officers, employees, corporate affiliates, agents or by any third party.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the requirement to promptly advise each other of unauthorized use or disclosure of confidential information to the section addressing execution of the agreement weakens its prominence. It obscures the critical duty to communicate breaches.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Notification obligations under the Privacy Act 1988 (Cth)
perturbation.law_explanation: By positioning the notification clause within procedural aspects of the agreement (execution), it lessens its impact and potential to be overlooked, thus contradicting the spirit and intention of prompt notification requirements as mandated by the Privacy Act 1988 (Cth) for data breaches.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Australian Privacy Principles quick reference | OAIC We use cookies on this site We use cookies to analyse traffic and to improve your browsing experience on our website. To find out more, read our privacy policy . Close Skip to main content Australian Privacy Principles quick reference Download the poster (PDF, 614 KB) Principle Title Purpose APP 1 Open and transparent management of personal information Ensures that APP entities manage personal information in an open and transparent way. This includes having a clearly expressed and up to date APP privacy policy . APP 2 Anonymity and pseudonymity Requires APP entities to give individuals the option of not identifying themselves, or of using a pseudonym. Limited exceptions apply. APP 3 Collection of solicited personal information Outlines when an APP entity can collect personal information that is solicited. It applies higher standards to the collection of sensitive information . APP 4 Dealing with unsolicited personal information Outlines how APP entities must deal with unsolicited personal information. APP 5 Notification of the collection of personal information Outlines when and in what circumstances an APP entity that collects personal information must tell an individual about certain matters. APP 6 Use or disclosure of personal information Outlines the circumstances in which an APP entity may use or disclose personal information that it holds. APP 7 Direct marketing An organisation may only use or disclose personal information for direct marketing purposes if certain conditions are met. APP 8 Cross-border disclosure of personal information Outlines the steps an APP entity must take to protect personal information before it is disclosed overseas. APP 9 Adoption, use or disclosure of government related identifiers Outlines the limited circumstances when an organisation may adopt a government related identifier of an individual as its own identifier, or use or disclose a government related identifier of an individual. APP 10 Quality of personal information An APP entity must take reasonable steps to ensure the personal information it collects is accurate, up to date and complete. An entity must also take reasonable steps to ensure the personal information it uses or discloses is accurate, up to date, complete and relevant, having regard to the purpose of the use or disclosure. APP 11 Security of personal information An APP entity must take reasonable steps to protect personal information it holds from misuse, interference and loss, and from unauthorised access, modification or disclosure. An entity has obligations to destroy or de-identify personal information in certain circumstances. APP 12 Access to personal information Outlines an APP entity’s obligations when an individual requests to be given access to personal information held about them by the entity. This includes a requirement to provide access unless a specific exception applies. APP 13 Correction of personal information Outlines an APP entity’s obligations in relation to correcting the personal information it holds about individuals. perturbation.justification: The modification moves the notification clause regarding unauthorized use or disclosure of confidential information from its original prominent position to a section dealing with the execution of the agreement. This structural change weakens the clause's prominence and potential to be noticed, as highlighted in the Perturbation Explanation. The Law Explanation suggests this contradicts the spirit and intention of prompt notification requirements under the Privacy Act 1988 (Cth). Snippet 2, referencing APP 11 (Security of personal information), states that an entity must take reasonable steps to protect personal information from unauthorized access or disclosure. Moving the notification clause to a less prominent position undermines 'reasonable steps' to ensure prompt notification, increasing the risk of delayed response to a security breach.
